coredata.dc:title: Highly biocompatible yogurt-derived carbon dots as multipurpose sensors for detection of formic acid vapor and metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Moonrinta, Sasaluck
Kwon, Binhee
In, Insik
Kladsomboon, Sumana
Sajomsang, Warayuth
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are fascinating nanomaterials given their straightforward synthesis, unique optical properties, sensing capabilities, and biocompatibility. In this work, biocompatible carbon dots were prepared from yogurt using a two-step pyrolysis/hydrothermal method. The dots were spherical in shape with an average size of 4.7 nm. They showed blue emission under UV illumination with a quantum yield of 1.5%. Their photoluminescence was stable over three months and in both strong buffer solutions and highly concentrated salt solutions. The optical absorption and photoluminescence properties of the dots were employed for vapor and metal ion sensing, respectively. For the first time, the carbon dots were integrated into an optical electronic nose, and used for the detection of formic acid vapor at room temperature. Sensing was based on monitoring the optical transmission through a carbon dot film upon exposure to vapor, and the results were confirmed by UV–visible spectroscopy. The carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was able to distinguish vapor from formic acid/water solutions at different concentrations, with a detection limit of 7.3% v/v. The sensitivity of the dots to metal ions was tested by measuring the photoluminescence emission intensity at different excitation wavelengths. Principal component analysis was used to differentiate between the ions. The results suggested that interactions between carbon dots and metals ions occurred at a range of binding sites. The biocompability of the dots was demonstrated to be excellent. The study identified carbon dots produced from yogurt as multipurpose fluorescent nanomaterials with potential sensing and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Yogurt
Metal ion sensor
Electronic nose
Formic acid vapor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Vapor sensing by optical electronic nose
Preparation and characterization of the carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the governmental budget through Thammasat University in the fiscal year 2017 (Contract No. 6/2560 ). S. Moonrinta is sponsored by the Thailand Graduate Institute of Science and Technology (TGIST: SCA-CO-2559-2322-TH ), National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA) . The authors acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC) , Division of Chemistry, Division of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon dots from yogurt.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS survey-scan spectrum, and XPS high-resolution scan in (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s regions of carbon dots.
(a) HR-TEM image, (b) histogram of hydrodynamic diameter from DLS characterization, (c) absorption and emission spectra, and (d) excitation-dependent emission spectra of carbon dots.
Fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dots in (a) NaCl, KCl, (b) PBS buffer solutions, (c) acidic and basic solutions, and (d) as a function of time.
Optical electronic nose used in this work.

(a) Gas sensing responses of carbon dot film upon exposure to formic acid/water vapors, (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots from formic acid (FA)/water (WT) vapor, (c) plot of sensor response and formic acid concentration in formic acid/water solutions, (d) gas sensing responses of carbon dot film upon exposure to formic acid/methanol (MA) vapors, (e) PCA two-dimensional score plots from formic acid/methanol vapors, and (f) plot of sensor response and formic acid concentration in formic acid/methanol solutions.

(a) UV–vis absorbance of carbon dot thin film in the presence of formic acid and methanol vapor and (b) schematic representation of strong optical absorption of carbon dots in the presence of formic acid.
(a) Fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dot solution in the presence of metal ions excited using five different wavelengths and (b) PCA score plots.
Relative viability of MDA-MB-213 and MDCK cells treated with carbon dots in the range 0.001–1 g L−1 for 24 h.
Cell viability of carbon dots from previous studies and this work.

head.ce:title: Highly biocompatible yogurt-derived carbon dots as multipurpose sensors for detection of formic acid vapor and metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT Convergence (Brain Korea PLUS 21), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 380-702, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of IT Convergence (Brain Korea PLUS 21)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 380-702, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Bangkok, 73170 Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Nanotechnology Center (NANOTEC), National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA), Thailand Science Park, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are fascinating nanomaterials given their straightforward synthesis, unique optical properties, sensing capabilities, and biocompatibility. In this work, biocompatible carbon dots were prepared from yogurt using a two-step pyrolysis/hydrothermal method. The dots were spherical in shape with an average size of 4.7 nm. They showed blue emission under UV illumination with a quantum yield of 1.5%. Their photoluminescence was stable over three months and in both strong buffer solutions and highly concentrated salt solutions. The optical absorption and photoluminescence properties of the dots were employed for vapor and metal ion sensing, respectively. For the first time, the carbon dots were integrated into an optical electronic nose, and used for the detection of formic acid vapor at room temperature. Sensing was based on monitoring the optical transmission through a carbon dot film upon exposure to vapor, and the results were confirmed by UV–visible spectroscopy. The carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was able to distinguish vapor from formic acid/water solutions at different concentrations, with a detection limit of 7.3% v/v. The sensitivity of the dots to metal ions was tested by measuring the photoluminescence emission intensity at different excitation wavelengths. Principal component analysis was used to differentiate between the ions. The results suggested that interactions between carbon dots and metals ions occurred at a range of binding sites. The biocompability of the dots was demonstrated to be excellent. The study identified carbon dots produced from yogurt as multipurpose fluorescent nanomaterials with potential sensing and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, also known as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots, are carbon-based nanomaterials widely used for applications in sensing [1], electronics [2], photocatalysis [3], and the biomedical field [1,4,5]. They were discovered in 2004 by Xu and coworkers during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes derived from arc-discharge soot [6]. Since then, carbon dots have emerged as promising photoluminescent nanoparticles, attracting broad attention in recent years because of their low toxicity [7], excellent photostability [8], and high water solubility [9]. In addition to their unique optical, structural, and chemical properties, carbon dots can be prepared by a range of methods, including laser ablation [10,11], arc discharge [6,12], electrochemical oxidation [13,14], and various thermal-based methods, such as pyrolysis [15,16], hydrothermal [5,17], and microwave techniques [18]. Furthermore, carbon dots can be synthesized from almost any carbon-containing precursors. Examples of precursors are food, wastes including watermelon peel [19], peanut shells [16], mango [20], garlic [21], ethanol [22], and alcohols [14]. These attractive properties have allowed carbon dots to be use in place of toxic semiconductor quantum dots [23], which general contain heavy metals such as cadmium [24] and lead [25].

A recent trend in the synthesis of carbon dots is the use of readily available raw materials as precursors that are cheap and abundant. In the current, carbon dots from yogurt were prepared using a two-step pyrolysis/hydrothermal method. Yogurt is the oldest and most popular fermented foods and often included in healthy diets as it contains important vitamins and minerals. It is also rich in calcium, proteins, fats, and carbohydrates. It is relatively cheap and readily available in most supermarkets. This makes yogurt an excellent choice as a precursor and source of heteroatoms for enhancing the quantum efficiency of carbon dots. Recently, Dinc et al. reported the use of carbon dots from yogurt, and demonstrated excellent biocompatibility in biomedical applications for cell imaging [26]. The goal of the current study was to investigate the sensing capacity of yogurt-derived carbon dots. Pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods were selected as they are ones of the most convenient and effective methods for synthesizing carbon dots.

Carbon dots obtain their multipurpose nanomaterial properties from the presence of surface functional groups. These groups have been linked to the unique optical properties of carbon dots. Interactions between the functional groups and small molecules or ions produce a change in the optical properties, allowing their use in sensing applications [27]. Carbon dots are thus regarded as excellent fluorescent materials and bio-probes [28,29]. However, the use of the optical absorption of carbon dots for sensing is extremely rare. The fabrication and use of multi-functional nanomaterials for various applications have attracted wide interests from the practical standpoints. To test the versatility of carbon dots as the multipurpose nanomaterials, we investigated the use of the as-prepared carbon dots for formic acid vapor and metal ion sensing, by measuring the optical absorption and emission upon exposure to those analytes. First, we developed a home-made optical electronic nose system with carbon dots as the sensing materials, and used them to sense formic acid vapor, which can be operated at room temperature. The electronic nose is a fast and convenient tool that mimics the human olfactory system. It is applied in many fields, such as environmental monitoring, food and beverage production, and medical applications [30–32]. It is capable of detecting changes in, for example, electrical, optical, or mass transductions depending on the type of detector used [33]. To take advantage of the unique optical properties of carbon dot film, our optical electronic nose system was designed to monitor a change in optical transmission upon exposure to chemical vapors. Formic acid was chosen as it is the carboxylic acid with the smallest molecules, very volatile, and is used as a precursor in the synthesis of many compounds. It is also used as a preservative and antibacterial agent. However, excessive exposure and accumulation can cause irritation, nerve damage, and blindness. Therefore, the ability to detect formic acid is necessary from the health and industrial standpoints. Liquid formic acid sensing can be performed using mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography, or gas chromatography [34,35]. However, sensors for formic acid vapor are rare. In 2001, Garcia-Verdugo-Caso et al. reported the use of a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor for the detection of formic acid vapor [36]. However, the QCM sensor uses a mass-based technique, and therefore suffers from low selectivity and slow response. Optical-based methods such as our optical electronic nose provide an alternative approach and offer excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and fast response. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report the use of carbon dots as a formic vapor sensor. Our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was able to distinguish vapor from formic acid/methanol mixtures at different concentrations, with the aid of principal component analysis (PCA).

The carbon dots were also tested as metal ion sensors. There has been considerable interest in the development of fast, simple, and effective sensors for detection of heavy metal ions. A number of analytical methods have been reported for the detection of metal ions, including high performance liquid chromatography [37], ion chromatography [38], atomic absorption spectroscopy [39], flame atomic absorption spectroscopy [40], mass spectroscopy [41], coupled plasma spectroscopy [42], capillary electrophoresis [43], and electron paramagnetic resonance [44]. However, these techniques require lengthy sample treatment and the use of sophisticated instruments. Fluorescence-based sensors offer unique advantages for metal ion detection because of their high sensitivity and selectivity, and low cost. They can also be used to create a fluorescent colorimetric paper-based device which is portable, simple, and cheap. Carbon dots are therefore one of the most studied fluorophores for the detection of heavy metal ions [17,45–47]. With the aid of PCA, we were able to distinguish several metal ions from a single set of measurements. We tested the cytotoxicity of the dots on normal and cancer cell lines, to show that they can be also used in biomedical applications. Our results demonstrated that one type of carbon dots synthesized from yogurt have potential uses in chemical sensing and biomedical applications.

Unflavored yogurt (Dutchie brand) was purchased from a local supermarket. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) was bought from Spectrum Labs. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments.

Yogurt (50 g) and hydrochloric acid (0.5 M, 25 mL) were mixed and placed in a 200-mL evaporating dish. The reaction mixture was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The resulting black solid was then added to a hydrochloric acid solution (0.5 M, 25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 8 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The solid was then rinsed with DI water and purified using the dialysis membrane for three days. The solution was then centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The carbon dot solution was freeze dried overnight to remove water, yielding carbon dots as a brown solid (0.0085 g, 0.02% yield).

The quantum yield (
Φ )
of carbon dots is measured using fluorescence spectroscopy. Quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The carbon dots were dissolved in DI water (n = 1.33) and the excitation wavelength of 373 nm was used. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (quantum yield of 54% at 349 nm, n = 1.33) [48]. The quantum yield was calculated as follows:
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
,
where I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance measured using a UV–visible spectrometer, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R denotes the reference sample.

We tested the selectivity of the dots to the following ions: Co2+ (CoCl2·6H2O), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Sn2+ (SnCl2), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2(SO4)2·6H2O), Cu2+(CuSO4·5H2O), Pb2+ (Pb(NO3)2), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), Na+ (Na2SO4), Fe3+ (FeCl3), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), Ag+ (AgNO3), Ca2+ (Ca(NO3)2), Cr6+ (K2Cr2O7), Cd2+ (Cd(NO3)2) and K+ (KNO3). Metal ion solutions (2 mL, 100 μM) and carbon dot solutions (4 mL, 0.1 g L−1) were mixed and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence emission spectra were then recorded using five excitation wavelengths: 320, 360, 400, 440, and 480 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vapor sensing by optical electronic nose
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dot film was prepared on a 1 × 1 inch glass slide by drop-casting a carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 30 g L−1). The film was then dried at 60 °C overnight. An optical electronic nose system comprises a light source, sensing materials, and a photo detector. Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and a commercial photo detector (ET-TCS230) were used to measure the light intensity. The eight gas sensor arrays used eight colors of LEDs: infrared, red (638 nm), yellow (587 nm), green (537 nm), blue (457 nm), blue-green (472 nm), violet (399 nm), and white. The light intensity transmitted through the sensing thin film was observed as photon counts, captured by the photo-detector [49]. All measurements were carried out at room temperature. The gas sensing of the thin films was examined under a dynamic gas flow through a home-made chamber. Nitrogen gas (99.99%) was used as the reference and carrier gas, at a flow rate of approximately 500 mL min−1 into the sensor chamber. The sensor system was switched between the sample and reference gases by a solenoid valve. Formic acid/water and formic acid/methanol solutions at different ratios were used as vapor sources.

Cytotoxicity was investigated using the MTT (3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. MDA-MB-231 cells and MDCK cell lines (all 200 μL) at a density of 1 × 105 cells per mL were placed in each well of a 96-well plate. The cells were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a 5% humidified CO2 atmosphere. To determine cell viability, stock solutions of carbon dots were dissolved in RPMI (Roswell Park Memorial Institute) medium at a concentration of 1 g L−1 and diluted to 0.001 g L−1. The medium was removed, and the cells were then treated with different concentrations of carbon dots and then incubated for an additional 24 h. Media containing carbon dots were then replaced with 180 μL of fresh medium and 20 μL of a stock solution containing 15 mg of MTT in 3 mL of PBS (phosphate buffered saline) and incubated for another 4 h. Finally, the medium was removed, 200 μL of MTT solubilizing agent was added to the cells, and the reaction mixture was shaken for 15 min. Absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader. The relative cell viability was measured by comparing the results with that of a control well containing cells only.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) and KBr pellets. The elemental composition of the carbon dots was investigated using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester, UK). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken on a TECNAI F20 system (Philips) at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dot aqueous solution (0.1 g L−1) were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco, FP-6200), respectively. The size and zeta potential of the carbon dot solutions (0.1 g L−1) were measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with a scattering angle of 173°. The electrode voltage for zeta potential measurement was 3.9 V. The refractive index, viscosity, and pH of the solution were 1.33, 0.895 mPa s, and 4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The dots were prepared in two-step: pyrolysis and a hydrothermal technique (Fig. 1 ). This was done to maximize the product yields and the quantum efficiency of the dots. The yogurt was pyrolyzed in the presence of hydrochloric acid and the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The dots were then purified using a standard procedure of filtering, dialysis, centrifugation, and freeze-drying. The carbon dot solution was clear under room lighting, but showed blue luminescence under UV excitation at 365 nm.

The functional groups and elemental composition of the dots were characterized using FTIR and XPS. The FT-IR spectrum showed characteristic vibrational signals of O–H/N-H at 3379 cm−1, C–H stretching vibration at 2932 cm−1, and C=O stretching vibration at around 1649 cm−1 (Fig. 2 a). This confirmed that the dots were strongly hydrophilic and water soluble. Hydrophilic functional groups were formed during synthesis from the oxidation reaction of yogurt under acidic conditions at high temperature. The C=C signal at 1555 cm−1 suggested the presence of sp2 carbons responsible for the conjugation within the carbon dot core. The XPS spectrum showed the signals of O1s, N1s, and C1s at 529 eV, 397 eV, and 282 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). The respective atomic concentrations were 27, 6, and 60%, showing carbon to be the majority component. The high-resolution spectrum of the C1s peak showed the presence of C-C, C=C, C-O, C-N, and O-C=O functional groups at 284.8 eV, 286.3, 288.0 eV, and 289.0 eV, indicating that the carbon dots comprised a range of oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups, consistent with the FT-IR result (Fig. 2c). The nitrogen was derived from the proteins and amino acids in the yogurt precursor. These functional groups are believed to be responsible for the unique hydrophilic and optical properties of carbon dots, as the photoluminescence is believed to arise from surface states [5,50]. These groups are crucial for sensing applications.

The size and morphology of carbon dots were determined using HR-TEM and DLS techniques. The HR-TEM image showed the dots to be spherical with a diameter of approximately 5–20 nm and agglomerated (Fig. 3 a). This agglomeration was possibly due to the hydrophilic nature of the dots, as reported in previous studies [51–54]. The lattice spacing of 0.4 nm, close to that of graphite, suggested the presence of an sp2 conjugated core within the dots [55]. The particle size was determined using DLS and found to be approximately 2–10 nm, with an average of 4.7 nm (Fig. 3b). This broad size distribution was possibly due to the agglomeration observed from HR-TEM image. The zeta potential of the carbon dots was −25.4 mV. The negative zeta potential confirmed that the carbon dots tended to aggregate and were negatively charged, possibly due to the presence of nitrogen-containing groups derived from the proteins and amino acids in the yogurt precursor.

The optical properties of the dots were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum exhibited a broad absorption band from 200 to 500 nm (Fig. 3c). Absorption peaks were observed at 282 and 333 nm, corresponding to π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of the C=O bonds, due to the presence of carbonyl-based groups on the surface [56]. The presence of C=C and C=O bonds was consistent with the FT-IR and XPS results. The fluorescence emission spectra measured as a function of excitation wavelength were generally broad, ranging from 300 to 700 nm, possibly due to the broad size distribution and a variety of the surface states (Fig. 3d). The emission was found to be excitation wavelength dependent, showing a maximum emission intensity at 340 nm. The excitation-dependent emission was also attributed to the difference in particle size and variation in emissive sites at the surface of the carbon dots [57]. The dots showed blue emission under 365 nm excitation with a quantum yield of 1.5%, confirming previous report by Dinc [26].

The photoluminescence stability of carbon dots in aqueous environments is of great interest when considering practical applications, as many factors, including pH and ionic strength, can affect their performance. First, the fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dots in salt, buffer, and aqueous solutions at different pH values was investigated. The intensity was minimally affected in NaCl, KCl, and PBS buffer solutions of different concentrations (Fig. 4 a and b), consistent with a previous report [58]. The emission of the carbon dots was only minimally altered by a change in pH (Fig. 4c). When placed in a highly basic solution (pH = 13), the fluorescence emission intensity decreased only minimally, possibly due to the destruction of some emissive states on the surface. Our carbon dots were shown to be stable in strongly acidic conditions. The stability of the photoluminescence of carbon dots as a function of time was also studied. We found that their photoluminescence was stable over 100 days. These results indicate that our carbon dots are sufficiently stable to be used in a range of harsh environments.

The unique optical properties of the carbon dots are due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups as binding sites for small molecules and ions and the large surface area. The dots are therefore promising as probes and are widely used in bio- and chemical sensing. When a chemical is adsorbed onto the surface of the dots, the optical properties change, allowing their use as sensors. This is mostly applied to solutions or solids, whereas the application of carbon dots for the detection of vapors or gases is less well developed. In this study, carbon dots were used as a sensing layer in an electronic nose system, for detection of formic acid vapor generated from formic acid solutions. An electronic nose mimics the human nose, and is widely used for monitoring of gases and vapors in environmental, food, clinical sensing, and military applications. There are several types of electronic nose that can be used to detect changes in electrical, optical, and mass transductions [33]. In this work, an optical electronic nose was used as carbon dots offer strong absorption in the UV and visible regions and excellent photostability prolonging the lifetime and supporting reproducibility. A typical optical electronic nose system comprises a sensing material, light source, photodetector, gas flow controller, and data acquisition unit (DAQ) (Fig. 5 ). Formic acid is the smallest carboxylic compound. This colorless and volatile liquid is widely used in consumer, pharmaceutical, and feedstock industries and as a building block in chemical synthesis. It is corrosive and an irritant, and can cause damage to the in optic nerve and central nervous system, and blindness. Detection of formic acid vapor is therefore essential from environmental, health, and industrial perspectives.

First, formic acid in aqueous solutions at several concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) was used as the vapor source, while nitrogen gas was used as the reference and carrier gas. The dynamic sensor responses of the film, corresponding to a change in the optical intensity passing through it, were monitored as a function of time under alternating flows of nitrogen gas and formic acid vapor (Fig. 6 a). The light transmission decreased dramatically when the flow was switched to formic acid, confirming that the carbon dots were sensitive to vapor. When the nitrogen reference gas was flowed through the film, the light transmission recovered to 95% of its original intensity after the film had been repeatedly flowed with N2 for 52 ± 5 s. This recovery time is among the best reported in the literature [59]. As the concentration of formic acid was reduced, the magnitude of the gas response decreased, and a negligible sensor response was observed when pure water was used as the vapor source. This confirmed that the dots were selectively sensitive to formic acid vapor, but not to water vapor. The switch of gases could be repeated multiple times. The carbon dots exhibited excellent repeatability as a sensing material, as one film that has been used for at least a hundred cycles showed no observable deterioration.

The sensor responses were subjected to principal component analysis (PCA). This is a statistical tool widely used for reducing high dimensional data into linearly uncorrelated variables (principal components, PCs) using orthogonal transformation. The PCA graphical output was obtained as score plots [60]. The sensor responses from the eight LEDs and five vapor sources were used as the input data. A circle in the PCA score plot was used to mark the exclusive zone of each source. The PCA results confirmed that the carbon dots were able to clearly differentiate formic acid vapor at different concentrations, and between formic acid and a pure water sample, without overlapping (Fig. 6b). We selected the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2), on which the carbon dots were able to discriminate formic acid with 89.2% of the total variability. The magnitude of sensor response to each vapor source was used to construct a plot, similar to a Stern-Volmer plot for a fluorescence quenching system. This plot showed a linear relationship between the sensor response and the formic acid concentration, indicating that the formic acid vapor played a role in modulating the optical transmission through the carbon dots (Fig. 6c). The detection limit, determined using the 3σ/slope method, was determined to be 7.3%. This result suggested that our use of PCA with a carbon dot-integrated electronic nose successfully detected formic acid vapor, and allowed its concentration in aqueous solutions to be quantified.

One of the most common industrial processes for the production of formic acid uses methanol as a precursor. We therefore tested the sensitivity of our electronic nose to formic acid-methanol mixtures (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) as vapor sources. The optical transmission of the film decreased dramatically when the flow was switched to the formic acid-methanol vapor. The sensor responses were stronger when the concentration of formic acid was increased, indicating a higher sensitivity of the dots to formic acid (Fig. 6d). The response (%) of the film to formic acid and methanol was further quantified using (IR- Ivapor)/IRx100, where Ivapor is the sensor response to the formic acid or methanol vapor and IR is the sensor response to the reference gas. The response to formic acid was about twice that to methanol (1.34% and 0.71%). The higher sensitivity to formic acid was attributed to the higher polarity of the acid, producing to stronger polar-polar and/or hydrogen-bonding interactions with the carbon dots. The sensor responses were again subjected to PCA. We found no overlap between the PCA score plots at different concentrations, and there accounted for 98.7% of total variability (Fig. 6e). The results suggested that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose can be used for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of mixture of formic acid and methanol. This will be useful for monitoring product yields during production, or identifying concentrations of formic acid/methanol. The magnitude of sensor response to each vapor was used to construct a plot between sensor response and formic acid concentration in the solutions. It was found that the sensor response increased as the formic acid concentration increased. However, the relationship between sensor response and formic acid concentration was not linear, possibly because of interference by the methanol vapor at low formic acid concentrations.

We next investigated the optical properties of the carbon film in the presence and absence of formic acid vapor. A thin film of carbon dots was prepared on a quartz slide and its absorbance was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy. An increase in absorbance was observed when the formic acid vapor was flowed through the film (Fig. 7 a), suggesting that the film absorbed more light in the presence of formic acid vapor. This was consistent with the optical electronic nose results in which the sensor response, i.e. light transmission, dropped in the presence of formic acid vapor. We thus confirmed that the change in absorbance of the carbon dots allowed them to sense the formic acid vapor. The change in absorbance was smaller than that shown by the electronic nose. This was attributed to the use of thinner film, to prevent saturation of absorbance. A similar result was observed for methanol vapor, but at a smaller magnitude. This was consistent with the electronic nose results, and confirmed that the carbon dots were more sensitive to formic acid vapor than to methanol vapor. The cause of the modulation in light absorption by the carbon dots in the visible range (400–800 nm) following adsorption of formic acid is unclear. It may be due to interactions between the polar formic acid molecules and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots and/or to the solvochromism effect, which produces a change in optical properties that can be monitored using UV–vis spectroscopy and an optical electronic nose [61,62]. The change in optical properties of the carbon dots upon exposure to VOCs could be due to a change in polarization around the dots, produced by the solvent molecules (Fig. 7b) [58,63]. Hypothesis that the polar-polar interaction and hydrogen bonding between the carbon dots and formic acid or methanol explain the change in absorption may be confirmed by transient spectroscopic and computational studies, which are underway.

In addition to vapor sensing, the carbon dots were used for the detection of metal cations: Co2+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ca2+, and K+. Carbon dots are well known probes for chemical and metal ions, due to their unique optical properties and surface functional groups. These surface functional groups, such as carboxylate and amino groups, are excellent chelating ligands for the formation of complexes with metal ions, changing their emissive states and photoluminescent intensity. These ions are commonly found in living organisms, the environment, foods, medicines, and supplements. In this experiment, metal ions and carbon dots were mixed in aqueous solution and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at five excitation wavelengths: 320, 360, 400, 440, and 480 nm (Fig. 8 a). The carbon dots produced a range of fluorescence responses towards the ions, which may be used to create unique fingerprints. Since our carbon dots are not selective to a single ion, the quenching percentages were subjected to principal component analysis (PCA). A circle was used to mark the exclusive zone of a kind of metal ion with the exception of Fe2+ and Ni2+, and the PCA score plots showed that all these metal ions could be separated from each other without error or misclassification, with a total variability of 99.99% (Fig. 8b). The eigenvalue of PC1 was 4.2, indicating a statistical significance, while the eigenvalues of the other PCs were less than 1. PCs with eigenvalues greater than 1 was considered as significant, based on the Kaiser rule [64]. The correlation was as follows: PC1 = 16%*λ 320nm + 21%*λ 360nm+23%*λ 400nm + 22%*λ 440nm + 18%*λ 480nm, indicating that these five excitation wavelengths made similar contributions to PC1. This result suggested that the interaction between carbon dots and metals ions occurs through binding sites or functional groups at the surface, and that each binding site is responsible for its own unique HOMO-LUMO energy level. This supports the finding that our carbon dots exhibit a range of surface functional groups, as shown by the FT-IR and XPS results, and are therefore sensitive to several metal ions. These results demonstrate clearly that our yogurt-derived carbon dots have potential application as a multimodal optical sensing platform for the detection and discrimination of metal ions.

In this work, carbon dots were prepared at relatively high yields from yogurt, a readily available precursor, using a simple two-step pyrolysis/hydrothermal method. We made use of the optical absorption of the modified carbon dots for formic acid vapor sensing, and of the photoluminescent properties for metal ion sensing. The dots showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity both the formic acid vapor and metal ions. When used in an optical electronic nose system, the dots were able to detect formic acid vapor generated from aqueous and methanolic solutions at room temperature. They were also able to differentiate between different types of metal ion, confirming that binding interactions between the dots and metal ions occurred at a range of binding sites. The photostability and biocompatibility of the carbon dots were excellent. The study confirmed that carbon dots prepared from yogurt have potential applications in sensing and biomedical devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the governmental budget through Thammasat University in the fiscal year 2017 (Contract No. 6/2560). S. Moonrinta is sponsored by the Thailand Graduate Institute of Science and Technology (TGIST: SCA-CO-2559-2322-TH), National Science and Technology Development Agency (NSTDA). The authors acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Division of Chemistry, Division of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University.
